title: How to Build & Sell AI Agents in 2026: Ultimate Beginner’s Guide
summary: A comprehensive tutorial on building and selling AI agents using n8n without coding experience. The guide covers four real-world AI agents including a Telegram expense tracker, website lead generation chatbot, voice agent with Retell AI, and a sales copilot with Lovable frontend, along with monetization strategies for launching an AI automation agency.
writeup: Liam Otley's full-course primer on AI agents covers the labor-market stakes, a precise agent-vs-chatbot distinction, and the five internal components that make an agent function, then transitions into build tutorials and a monetization blueprint. The through-line is that agents are genuine autonomous workers rather than information-retrieval tools: they act on external systems (calendars, databases, email) rather than returning text. McKinsey and World Economic Forum figures frame the urgency, while the component walkthrough (LLM, prompt, memory, tools, orchestration) gives the structural vocabulary needed before touching a no-code builder.
key_points: McKinsey's November 2025 report found 57% of current American work is automatable with existing technology, and the World Economic Forum projects 92 million jobs displaced by 2030; simultaneously, workers with AI skills earn 56% more than peers, a gap that doubled in one year, while only 12% of workers have taken any AI training.

The defining contrast between a chatbot and an agent is action versus information: a chatbot can only return text describing available appointment slots, while an agent checks the live calendar, books the slot, sends a confirmation email, and updates the scheduling system, all autonomously in seconds.

An AI agent is composed of five parts: an LLM (the reasoning brain, e.g., GPT-5, Claude, Gemini), a prompt (plain-language instructions that determine behavior without requiring code), memory, tools that connect the agent to external systems, and an orchestration layer that sequences actions.

Satya Nadella (Microsoft), Jensen Huang (Nvidia), and Sundar Pichai (Google) all stated publicly that 2026 is the inflection year for agentic AI moving from experimentation to widespread deployment, making the skill window unusually time-sensitive.

Building and deploying an agent maps directly onto hiring an employee: define the role and responsibilities (the prompt), grant access to relevant systems (the tools), and the agent operates independently around the clock at a fraction of human labor cost.
